logical_blocks.content: The evolution of the Internet Protocol (IP): From IPv4 to IPv6 and the Future of the Global Network. The development of the global communication network that we know today as the Internet rests on one fundamental pillar: the IP (Internet Protocol). It is IP that is responsible for addressing and routing data packets, enabling communication between devices around the world. As the number of devices connected to the network grew at an avalanche pace, this infrastructure had to constantly evolve. The following text provides a detailed overview of this transformation,, with particular emphasis on the history, successive versions, key dates, and statistics that illustrate the incredible scale and pace of world digitization. Origins and the birth of version 4 (IPv4). The history of modern network addressing officially begins in the early 1980s. Although earlier experiments, such as the NCP (Network Control Program) protocol in the ARPANET network, paved the way for new technologies, it was September 1981 that brought an absolute breakthrough with the publication of the technical document RFC 791. This document defined the standard that we know today as IPv4 (Internet Protocol version 4). Interestingly, version numbers 1 through 3 were purely experimental versions and never made it into widespread commercial use. The day that ultimately sealed the fate of the digital world was January 1, 1983. This date, often called the "birth of the Internet," marks the moment of the complete transition of the ARPANET network from the NCP protocol to the TCP/IP stack? The IPv4 architecture, is based on 32-bit addresses, which allows the generation of exactly 4,294,967,296 unique IP addresses. In the first half of the 1980s, this seemed like an inexhaustible pool. It was estimated at the time that global demand for addresses would use only a small fraction of this pool – forecasts assumed the use of less than 5,1 % of available resources in the first decade of the standard's existence. The address space crisis and the "lost" version 5.. Over the years, with the true explosion in popularity of the commercial Internet in the 1990s, and then the emergence of the first smartphones, the pool of IPv4 addresses began to melt at an alarming rate. By the beginning of the new millennium, analysts were warning that we would soon run out of free IP numbers to assign to new devices. So why wasn't IPv5 introduced. Version 5 of the IP protocol (known in the engineering community as the Internet Stream Protocol, ST) actually existed and was developed in the late 1970s and 1980s. However, it was reserved exclusively for experiments with video and voice transmission (the prototype of today's VoIP). Because it was never intended to replace standard IP traffic, the new main protocol was given the next available version number – Version 6. To postpone the space exhaustion crisis, NAT (Network Address Translation) technology was introduced on a massive scale. Thanks to it, entire corporate and home local networks could hide behind a single public IP address, which temporarily saved the network architecture. Despite these efforts, on January 31, 2011, the main organization managing the pool (IANA) officially issued the last five large /8 blocks to regional Internet registries. In November 2019, the European registry RIPE NCC announced the complete exhaustion of its pool, stating that 100% of the 4.3 billion IPv4 addresses entrusted to it had finally been allocated. Address trading and the economic aspect. Due to the lack of new addresses after 2011, a new phenomenon emerged on the secondary market – commercial IP address trading. In 2015, the average market price for a single IPv4 address was around $6 USD. By the end of 2021, this price had risen drastically, reaching a ceiling of over $50 USD per piece, representing a dramatic increase of over 730% in less than six years. 135.444,17. 144,444.17
